one_line_takeaway: Eraser reframes jailbreak defense as knowledge removal: it unlearns harmful answers via gradient ascent (simulating jailbreak prompts with random affixes) while KL-distilling to retain entity knowledge and refusal behavior, cutting jailbreak ASR by up to ~40 points on Llama2-7b with no loss of general benchmark accuracy.
key_finding: Eraser sharply cuts jailbreak success while essentially preserving general capability, achieving a better harmlessness/usefulness trade-off than baselines. On AdvBench it reduces AIM ASR from 19.61% to 0.57%, AutoDAN from 24.61% to 2.88%, and GCG from 40.57% to 8.26%; on AdvExtent (unseen same-topic queries) it reduces AIM from 23.74% to 0.04%, AutoDAN from 44.36% to 5.99%, GCG from 17.74% to 1.67%, showing generalization to unseen harmful topics of the same theme. Meanwhile its average benchmark accuracy is 63.76% vs the base model's 63.59% (RSFT 61.31%, GAM 62.27% both degrade). A striking secondary finding: simply unlearning random (semantically meaningless) token sequences via gradient ascent also yields defensive gains (GA w/ random cuts AIM ASR to 5.38%), but only by degrading general ability (63.59% to 61.65%) - and when Eraser's capability-retention objectives are applied on top of random data, defense collapses back to baseline (Eraser w/ random AIM ASR 19.03%). This shows a large part of naive-unlearning 'defense' is really a harmlessness-usefulness trade-off from broken instruction-following, and that Eraser's genuine defense comes specifically from forgetting harmful content, not from capability degradation.
core_contribution: Eraser is a post-hoc jailbreaking defense for already-aligned LLMs that treats the root cause of jailbreaks as the harmful knowledge still residing in model weights, and removes it via machine unlearning. Unlike prior filtering or continued-training defenses that only teach the model to reject harmful inputs, Eraser performs gradient ascent to make the model forget harmful answers while explicitly counterbalancing the collateral damage unlearning causes. It jointly optimizes three objectives: (1) unlearn harmful knowledge (Lf, gradient ascent on harmful answers, wrapped in a T(.) function that adds random prefixes/suffixes each epoch to simulate jailbreak prompts); (2) retain general knowledge (Lh, KL-distillation from the pre-unlearning model on auto-generated entity-comprehension questions built from entities appearing in the harmful answers); (3) maintain safety alignment (Lr, KL-distillation to preserve refusal behavior). The combined loss L = Max(0, gamma + Lf) + Lh + Lr caps the unlearning term at threshold gamma so the model stops forgetting once 'sufficient' and then keeps restoring general/refusal ability. A notable design point is that Eraser does not need the model's own harmful knowledge or a red team: harmful answers to unlearn are drawn from a public uncensored model (Wizard-Vicuna-30B-Uncensored), on the intuition that different answers to the same harmful question overlap enough that forgetting one generalizes.
threat_model: Attacker has access to a safety-aligned LLM (Llama2-chat-7b) that refuses direct harmful queries, and uses jailbreak prompts to bypass safeguards and elicit harmful content. The paper evaluates three jailbreak attack methods: AIM (a hand-crafted role-play prompt, top-voted on jailbreakchat.com), AutoDAN (hierarchical genetic-algorithm search for jailbreak prompts, Liu et al. 2023), and GCG (gradient-based white-box adversarial suffix, Zou et al. 2023). The defender's goal is to make the model refuse harmful queries under these attacks while preserving general capability and helpfulness. Eraser assumes the target model has already undergone safety alignment (it is a post-hoc defense, not an alignment replacement).
limitations: By the authors' own statement: Eraser only defends against the specific harmful topics it unlearns, and enumerating all harmful topics is intractable, so it cannot drive ASR to 0% (the forgotten set does not cover all harmful knowledge in the base model). It is applicable only to LLMs that have already undergone safety alignment and is not a replacement for RLHF-style alignment. Other reviewer-noted constraints: all main experiments are on a single 7B model (Llama2-chat-7b), so scaling/generalization across model families is untested; the harmfulness score and ASR both depend on a GPT-3.5 judge, and the ASR threshold (harmfulness > 2) is the authors' own choice; harmful answers are sourced from one specific uncensored model; and the AdvExtent test set and its 'unseen' status are constructed by the same authors from AdvBench topics.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) - 520 harmful queries; used for training and testing
AdvExtent - authors' new test set of 417 samples from 139 AdvBench harmful topics x 3 new prompts, to test same-topic generalization
Wizard-Vicuna-30B-Uncensored - public uncensored model used to generate the harmful answers to be unlearned
Alpaca (Taori et al. 2023) - 100 benign instructions used for latency/refusal-rate evaluation of RA-LLM (appendix)
General-capability benchmarks: Arc_challenge, Arc_easy, Copa, Cb, HendrycksTest, Boolq, Hellaswag
quantitative_results: AdvBench AIM ASR: base 19.61% -> Eraser 0.57% (RSFT 0.00%, GAM 30.00%)
AdvBench AutoDAN ASR: base 24.61% -> Eraser 2.88% (RSFT 2.88%, GAM 32.30%)
AdvBench GCG ASR: base 40.57% -> Eraser 8.26% (RSFT 9.61%, GAM 15.00%)
AdvExtent AIM ASR: base 23.74% -> Eraser 0.04%; AutoDAN 44.36% -> 5.99%; GCG 17.74% -> 1.67%
Average accuracy over 7 benchmarks: base 63.59%, Eraser 63.76%, RSFT 61.31%, GAM 62.27%
Ablation (AIM, AdvBench): Eraser ASR 0.57% / general 63.76%; Eraser w/o T(.) ASR 3.84%; pure GA ASR 0.00% but general drops to 62.24%
Defense-source test (AIM): GA w/ random-token unlearning ASR 5.38% (general 61.65%); Eraser w/ random-token ASR 19.03% (general 63.61%, defense lost)
Harmfulness scores (1-5): AdvBench AIM base 1.68 -> Eraser 1.03; AutoDAN 1.90 -> 1.09; GCG 2.78 -> 1.33
Appendix RA-LLM: AdvBench AIM ASR 6.92% and AutoDAN 5.96% (vs Eraser 0.57% / 2.88%); RA-LLM latency 11.53s and 8.00% benign refusal vs Eraser 6.47s and 0.00% refusal
Similar-but-harmless topic refusal rate (150 questions): base 8.00%, Eraser 8.66%, GAM 45.63%, RSFT 48.99%
baselines_compared: RSFT (Deng et al. 2023) - rejection SFT on collected jailbreak prompts; lr 1e-4, weight decay 1e-3
GAM (Yao et al. 2023) - general LLM unlearning applied to the aligned model
RA-LLM (Cao et al. 2023) - robust-alignment-check filtering method (appendix comparison)
Base model - Llama2-chat-7b with no additional defense
